title: ‘No surprises’: Behind IAG’s pitch to take risk out of insurance
full_text:
Nick Hawkins recently notched up five years heading up Insurance Australia Group, but he’s not ready to do a lap of honour just yet. He’s preparing for the coming years, including an investor day on Tuesday where he plans to lay out a new strategy under Ambition 2030.
Whether Hawkins remains in charge to see it all the way through is a matter for chairman Tom Pockett, the former Woolworths finance boss. However, the career insurance executive is determined to keep IAG on a growth path.
The way Hawkins sees it, the heavy structural lifting has been done at the nation’s biggest insurer that sits behind state-based names like NRMA, RACV and more recently RACQ. There’s plenty of room for organic growth, fine tuning and to drive the insurance engine even harder.
“We rebased the company five years ago. We’ve got ourselves set. We’re very focused on the way we run it. We divided up and separated out the retail and commercial businesses. The strategy for the next five years is about continuing down the same path,” Hawkins tells The Australian.
“In my mind, there’s a lot of opportunities.”
Hawkins has passed the key goals he set himself five years ago. On the last set of accounts IAG’s return on equity came in at 14.9 per cent (Hawkins’ target was 12 per cent to 13 per cent). His commercial insurance profit of $328m last year came in above the $250m target, and he hit his goal set out in 2021 of adding one million new customers to the business. He hasn’t hit the stretch target of 80 per cent of claims lodged digitally yet, but the next wave of technology will change that.
Hawkins sees the distribution of insurance increasingly being up-ended by AI. In coming years it’s likely that customers will have their own AI agents scanning the market for insurance. He wants IAG to be ready to meet that challenge.
There’s also upheaval in the back end. For hundreds of years, insurers have been using data in various forms to make predictions of risk and price policies around that. AI promises to dramatically speed things up.
“Imagine how many photographs of motor vehicles we have? Millions. We’re already deploying technology that allows us to scan those and assess damage or total loss very quickly. We can automate the whole process, but that’s a good example where we’re deploying technology right now.”
Hawkins says IAG has long moved away from its accident-prone past, where it was a multitude of retail and commercial business lines working on their own platforms and often buying their own reinsurance. This model meant frequent nasty shocks for investors. Getting the business onto a single tech platform was a key move in eliminating “operational risk” inside the business. At the same time, getting the right reinsurance structures in place (including a landmark agreement with Warren Buffett’s Berkshire Hathaway) turned IAG into a low volatility, capital light business.
“We’ve got our own house in order,” he says.
Even as a mature insurer, there’s plenty of organic growth in the Australian and New Zealand market, which he reckons can grow at 6 per cent.
He acknowledges recent acquisitions of RACQ and now Western Australia’s RAC are exceptions to this, although he maintains both were highly opportunistic as the local motoring clubs were looking for the right partner to serve insurance needs. (The proposed $1.35bn RAC WA deal has been contested by the competition regulator and is now moving into Phase 2 review under the new approval rules).
Hawkins won’t talk about that transaction beyond saying it is good for WA RAC members. “We’re maintaining the heritage of the West Australian mutual with the capability of IAG.”
His line is to push the concept of one insurance company. Within that there are two different businesses: home and auto; and commercial. But the aim is to keep insurance simple and not try to become all things in financial services like life, health or other insurance. “Otherwise you average things out too much and you’re not best in class.
“I really want to be best in class here.”
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
Document AUSTOL0020260507em570038s
summary: Nick Hawkins, CEO of Insurance Australia Group (IAG), outlines a new strategy, Ambition 2030, focusing on organic growth and technological advancements in insurance. IAG has improved its operational efficiency and aims to leverage AI for risk assessment and customer engagement. The company is positioned for growth in the Australian and New Zealand markets, despite regulatory challenges surrounding recent acquisitions.

heading: Risk Management
key_points: IAG's strategy includes a focus on organic growth and technology integration.
The company has achieved key financial targets, including a return on equity of 14.9%.
Hawkins aims to simplify insurance offerings while maintaining a strong focus on core business areas.
relevance: This is relevant to actuaries and financial services professionals as it highlights the evolving landscape of insurance and the importance of technology in risk management and customer service.
